items.title: Burkina: le District 403 A3 en ordre de marche aux côtés de l’Etat
items.description: Une forte délégation conduite par le Gouverneur du District 403 A3, Arouna Nikiema, a été reçue, le 20 juillet 2026, par le Directeur de Cabinet du ministre de l'Education de Base, de l'Alphabétisation et de la Promotion des Langues Nationales (MEBAPLN). Cette rencontre visait à ancrer l'action des Lions et Léos du Burkina dans la politique nationale de l'éducation. Les responsables du District 403 A3 du Lions club international pour le compte du mandat 2026-2027, ont rencontré, le lundi 20 juillet 2026, les représentants du ministère de l'Education de Base, de l'Alphabétisation et de la Promotion des Langues Nationales (MEBAPLN) en vue du renforcement de leurs partenariats. La délégation de l'association engagée aux côtés des couches les plus vulnérables de la société a été conduite par le Gouverneur, Arouna Nikiema. Le Gouverneur du District 403 A3, Arouna Nikièma Au cœur des échanges, il y a « l'harmonisation de la vision et des actions du mandat 2026-2027 avec les priorités » du MEBAPLN dans une logique de synergie et de complémentarité au service des communautés, selon un écrit partagé sur le réseau social Facebook du District. A cette occasion, le Gouverneur Nikiema a présenté les présidents des commissions en charge des actions liées à l'éducation et leurs plans d'actions. Une délégation du Lions club international reçue par les autorités éducatives du Burkina Faso Le Directeur de Cabinet a félicité les Lions pour leur « contribution à l'atteinte des objectifs nationaux ». Il les encourage à poursuivre leurs actions qui apporte un soulagement aux communautés bénéficiaires. « Quand l'engagement lion rencontre la volonté institutionnelle, c'est tout un secteur qui avance » avec le nouveau Gouverneur dont le solgan est: « Osons l'impossible ! ». Photo de famille à la fin de l'entrevue Après la cérémonie de passation de charge entre le Gouverneur du District 403 A3, sortant Yacouba Barry et l'entrant Arouna Nikiema, qui a eu lieu le 14 juillet 2026, une autre cérémonie grandiose est annoncée à Ouagadougou, le samedi 25 juillet prochain, pour l'installation officielle de Arouna Nikiema. Par Wakat Séra L’article Burkina: le District 403 A3 en ordre de marche aux côtés de l'Etat est apparu en premier sur Wakat Séra .

items.title: Cour d’Appel de Kaolack: Quatre nouveaux juges suppléants prêtent serment !
items.description: La Cour d'Appel de Kaolack a accueilli,ce mercredi, la prestation de serment de quatre nouveaux juges suppléants issus de la promotion 2024-2026 du Centre de formation judiciaire (CFJ). Lors de cette audience solennelle, présidée par le premier président Waly Diouf, en présence du procureur général Youssoupha Diallo et du représentant du bâtonnier de l'Ordre des avocats du Sénégal, Me El Hadji Malick Diouf, les nouveaux magistrats se sont engagés à exercer leurs fonctions avec impartialité, intégrité et dans le respect des lois. Selon le procureur général, leur arrivée permettra de combler plusieurs postes vacants et de renforcer les capacités de la juridiction. Il a également insisté sur les exigences de la profession, invitant les jeunes magistrats à faire preuve de rigueur, de sagesse et d'humanité. Au nom du bâtonnier, Me El Hadji Malick Diouf a rappelé que le serment constitue un engagement permanent qui doit guider toute leur carrière. Clôturant la cérémonie, le premier président Waly Diouf a souhaité aux nouveaux juges une brillante carrière, les exhortant à accomplir leur mission avec probité, responsabilité et fidélité au serment prêté. Mamadou THIAM (Correspondant)

items.title: Malaysia won’t be intimidated over Israelis’ entry ban, Anwar tells US lawmakers
items.description: Malaysia would not be intimidated into changing its decades-old policy of not allowing Israeli nationals into the country, Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim said on Wednesday. Anwar said Malaysia was also not in contravention of any international laws for having such a policy in place. “I would like to respond to the United States lawmakers, some of them at least, who stated categorically that Malaysia is in contravention of international laws and was disrespectful of the rights of nations. “This is...

items.title: Newly sworn-in GB CM picks 8 PPP, 4 IPP assembly members to join his cabinet as ministers
items.description: GILGIT: Gilgit-Baltistan’s newly sworn-in Chief Minister Amjad Hussain has selected 12 members to join his cabinet as ministers, it emerged on Wednesday, following the issuance of a notification for the cabinet members’ appointment. According to the notification, seen by Dawn , 12 individuals from Hussain’s own party, the PPP, and four from the Istehkam-i-Pakistan Party (IPP) have been named as members of the new cabinet. The notification, issued by the Services and General Administration Department and Cabinet Department, stated that on the advice of the chief minister, the GB governor “has been pleased to appoint the following members of the Gilgit-Baltistan Assembly as ministers to the government of Gilgit-Baltistan”. The notification then lists the members, who include: Zulfiqar Ali Murad (PPP) Syed Jalal Shah (PPP) Muhammad Ali Akhtar (PPP) Naiknam Karim (PPP) Iqbal Hassan (PPP) Raja Nasir Khan (PPP) Muhammad Naseem (PPP) Syed Tauqeer Mehdi (PPP) Aman Ali (IPP) Asad Shafiq (IPP) Anwar Ali (IPP) Muhammad Dilpazeer (IPP) The notification stated that the selected ministers would be assigned portfolios separately and the governor would administer the oath to them. The four IPP members picked for the cabinet had contested the June 7 elections in GB as independent candidates and later joined their current party. Currently, the GB Assembly comprises 14 members from the PPP, nine from the PML-N, seven from the IPP, one from the Majlis Wahdat-i-Muslimeen (MWM) and one PTI-backed independent candidate. Meanwhile, a by-election is due to be held for one of the seats, GBA-9 Skardu-III, on September 5. The GB Election Commission announced the by-election earlier this month after the GB Supreme Appellate Court disqualified PPP candidate Fida Muhammad Nashad for concealing his assets in his nomination papers. Following the elections, the PML-N and the IPP had decided to support the PPP in the elections for the chief minister, speaker and deputy speaker. However, it was agreed that the PML-N would sit in the opposition.

items.title: PPP chairman calls upcoming AJK elections 'most significant' in history
items.description: PPP Chairman Bilawal Bhutto Zardari on Wednesday said the upcoming election in Azad Jammu and Kashmir (AJK) was perhaps the “most significant” in the region’s history, saying its future was tied to the outcome. Addressing a rally in Mirpur, the PPP chairman said, “I do not consider this election campaign and these elections to be ordinary. Perhaps, this is the most significant election in the history of AJK.” “The decisions you make in this election will determine the future of Azad Jammu and Kashmir,” he added. Bilawal said former prime ministers and PPP leaders — Zulfikar Ali Bhutto and Benazir Bhutto — along with President Asif Ali Zardari, had made history by securing the constitutional rights of AJK, adding that it was now his turn to leave his mark on history. “I want history to remember that Bilawal Bhutto-Zardari and the PPP gave the people of AJK the right to self-governance, the right to employment and the right to determine their own future.” The PPP scion added that he had promised the people of Gilgit-Baltistan the right to self-governance, the right to employment and greater self-rule, and had won the election on that manifesto. Referring to AJK, the PPP chairman said he had brought the same manifesto to the region. In simpler terms, he said he wanted the people of Kashmir to enjoy the same rights as those available to the people of Pakistan in other regions. “We have been struggling for the right to self-government for AJK globally for three generations; now, we have to advance this struggle for self-government within Pakistan,” he stated. He stressed that he would ensure the right to self-government for AJK while also defending its narrative at international forums. “It is 100 per cent possible that we give rights to the people of AJK and also continue their struggle for freedom and advocate their case at international forums,” Bilawal said. Bilawal said the PPP was the only party that sought justice for the 12 refugee seats. “When rigging occurs on those 12 seats and people think that they have those seats in their pockets, they do injustice to you.” He further stated that he did not agree with those who argued against refugee representation or giving voting rights to refugees, adding: “I say give the vote to refugees, give them representation, but Punjab or Sindh will not decide for Kashmir; only Kashmir will decide for Kashmir.” He vowed that if the people of AJK gave the PPP a two-thirds majority in the upcoming election, the party would bring an amendment to the AJK Constitution to ensure justice for both the people of AJK and refugees. “Is it not possible that we retain AJK’s status as a state and keep its own prime minister, but ensure that AJK’s voice reaches Islamabad?” Bilawal said. The PPP chairman said AJK should have representation in Pakistan’s decision-making bodies on an observer or provisional basis so that it could raise its issues there. “Instead of protesting on roads, you should raise your concerns in the Centre; this is my manifesto.” He also recalled that he had proposed the formation of a truth and reconciliation commission to resolve the ongoing issues in AJK. “Neither the government nor the protesters have responded to this proposal yet. In our opinion, this is the only solution to this problem.” Commenting on the suspension of internet services in AJK amid the protests, Bilawal said it was “oppressive”. Moreover, he said he had instructed the AJK PPP president to write a letter to the AJK Election Commission, questioning the transparency of the elections, as political parties were unable to run campaigns on social media, and urging the federal government to restore internet services. The AJK Election Commission had announced that general elections in the region would be held on July 27. The elections would be held in three phases , ending on August 10.

items.title: Rain-related fatalities during ongoing monsoon spell rise to 20 in KP
items.description: LOWER SOUTH WAZIRISTAN/ MIRANSHAH: The death toll from rain-related incidents during the ongoing monsoon spell and flash floods in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa has risen to 20. According to the Pakistan Meteorological Department (PMD), the current rain spell, which began on July 19, is expected to last till July 23 in KP. The latest rain-related fatalities in the province were reported from North Waziristan, where two men drowned after being swept away by a flash flood in the Tochi River, Rescue 1122 spokesperson Noor Islam said in a statement. He said the drowned men’s bodies were recovered from the river after hours-long efforts. The spokesperson warned that the rains had resulted in “exceptionally” high water levels in the Tochi River, where flood-like conditions persisted. He urged area residents to stay at a distance from rivers, seasonal streams and water channels, stressing that water levels could rise within minutes due to flash floods. “Rescue 1122 emergency teams remain on high alert and are deployed at various locations to respond to any emergency,” the spokesperson said. He also advised people not to risk their lives by attempting to collect firewood, livestock or other belongings during floods. Earlier, four people were killed in a roof collapse incident in Upper South Waziristan, Additional Deputy Commissioner Fazal Wadood told Dawn . He said the incident occurred in the Bangiwala village on the outskirts of Sararogha tehsil during heavy rainfall. The victims belonged to the same family, including a couple and their son and daughter, he added. A report released by the PDMA this morning, which did not account for the incidents in North Waziristan, said that 18 people had died and 19 had been injured in rain-related incidents across the province since July 19. The authority’s report said 23 houses had been partially damaged in rain-related incidents and seven destroyed. A total of 13 cattle perished during the four days. Loss of lives and property due to rain-related incidents across KP from July 19 to July 22, 2026. — screengrab from PDMA report In a separate statement, the KP PDMA said that water levels and flows in all rivers and streams across the province were currently normal, and all district administrations had been directed to remain on alert. It added that people may call the helpline 1700 for information about weather conditions or in case of any untoward incident. Landslides block Wana-Gomal Zam Road Heavy rains have also disrupted routine life in Lower South Waziristan, with landslides blocking the Wana-Gomal Zam Road — one of the most important trade and communication routes in the region. There are also reports of flash floods damaging agricultural land in several areas. The key route is said to have been in disrepair since February , with residents describing it as virtually unusable last month, before the monsoon season even arrived. A district administration official confirmed the road blockage to Dawn on Wednesday following landslides. Several residents and commuters told Dawn over the phone on Wednesday that large boulders had fallen from mountains at several points along the road, while deep potholes had developed at various locations. They said several culverts, bridges and protection walls had also been damaged, making travel difficult and risky for motorists. They called on the authorities to take immediate steps to reopen the road and restore traffic. Later, Lower South Waziristan Communication and Works Engineer Ikram Wazir told Dawn that an operation to clear the road had been initiated, in which tractors were being employed. The official said the road had been severely damaged at several locations due to intense flooding. “The restoration work is currently under way,” he said, estimating that the road would be reopened for traffic by Thursday. Meanwhile, Ahmadzai Wazir tribal elders residing in the region, while speaking to Dawn , urged Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif and other relevant authorities to take immediate measures for the reconstruction of the Wana-Gomal Zam Road. The elders said the road had been in disrepair for a long time and was often closed even after moderate rainfall because of its poor condition. In another development, there were also reports of flash floods in various areas of Lower South Waziristan. District administration officials told Dawn that high flows were reported in the Tanai and Spin streams following the rainfall. Govt reviews monsoon preparedness Separately, Planning Minister Ahsan Iqbal posted on X that he chaired the third meeting of the Emergency Response Committee at the National Emergencies Operations Centre (NEOC) on Wednesday to review the country’s preparedness for the ongoing monsoon season. The minister said the meeting, attended by National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) Chairman Lt Gen Inam Haider Malik and representatives of relevant federal ministries, reviewed provincial preparedness, relief supplies and drainage systems in detail. “The NEOC is monitoring the weather situation round the clock, and I have issued strict instructions to all departments, including the Water and Power Development Authority, […] to remain on high alert,” the minister said. “Our utmost priority is to protect the life of every citizen, safeguard vulnerable areas, and ensure swift relief operations in any emergency situation,” he said.

items.title: The unregulated crocodile
items.description: In June, people in Hasafa village in Qalyubiya shared photos and videos on social media of a small crocodile, about a meter long, roaming through a drainage canal. A team from the Environment Ministry was dispatched to catch it, and the ministry later said the crocodile would be taken to Aswan and released into its “natural habitat in Lake Nasser.” A few days later, another larger crocodile was spotted sliding from a muddy bank into an irrigation canal near the village of Shabramant in Aboul Nomros in Giza. Again, an Environment Ministry team located it and transferred it to one of the ministry’s facilities. The successive sightings of Nile crocodiles so far north along the river raises questions over how they got there. Since the Aswan High Dam was completed in 1970, the species has been found almost exclusively in Lake Nasser, as changes to their natural habitat had already reduced their movement beyond the lake. In its statement about the Hasafa crocodile, the ministry suggested that someone had thrown the animal into the canal, pointing to the illegal possession and trade of crocodiles. Ekramy al-Abassiry, the general director of the South Egypt sector of protected areas and the head of the Crocodile Management Unit at the Egyptian Environmental Affairs Agency in Aswan, offers the same explanation. Young crocodiles, he says, are taken from around Lake Nasser shortly after hatching, transported to northern governorates and sold as pets, only to be abandoned once they grow too large to handle. The possession or transport of crocodiles — whether alive or dead — is illegal, Abassiry says. That is why many people who keep the animals avoid handing them over to the authorities once they grow too large and instead release them into public canals. The crocodiles’ journey from Lake Nasser to northern Egypt has been accompanied by a shift in how people relate to them. Periods of coexistence were common before crocodiles were hunted for touristic and commercial purposes, despite the legal ban. Officials have repeatedly proposed regulating crocodile hunting in a way that would generate economic returns, without reaching a solution. Before crocodiles became a commercial commodity in the second half of the 1990s, fishers around Lake Nasser hunted them for attacking fish within licensed fishing areas and damaging nets and fishing tents, Nabil Mansour, a fisher on the lake and a former candidate to lead the Aswan Fishers Syndicate tells Mada Masr. The second half of the 1990s brought exceptional floods after years of drought , pushing Lake Nasser’s water levels to unprecedented highs. Water flowing from Sudan carried some crocodiles into the southern part of the lake, while flooding expanded the shallow waters and flood control channels that make ideal breeding grounds for crocodiles, according to officials and researchers who spoke to Al-Wafd during its coverage of the 1997 floods. As the floods expanded the crocodiles’ habitat, encounters with people around the lake became more frequent. Between 1996 and 1998, several fatal attacks were recorded, resulting in the death of a laborer, a fisher, a mechanic at the Aswan High Dam dock and a shepherd from the Bishari and Ababda tribes. Another man survived after abandoning his camel to a crocodile. As complaints about large crocodiles mounted, the military deployed snipers around the dam’s dock during the 1998 floods to kill the animals, as they were considered a threat to workers, fishers and local residents. The attacks opened the debate over allowing crocodile hunting to reduce their numbers, amid concerns about their impact on nearby communities and fish populations — concerns that officials and specialists continued to dismiss . At the same time, proposals for crocodile farms began to emerge, investments were announced , and studies were launched into the feasibility of raising crocodiles. Reducing crocodiles to little more than a threat to people and fish overlooks a much longer history of coexistence with the communities around Lake Nasser. Even during years when crocodile numbers swelled with the floods, they never reached the point of devastating fish populations or preying on people, a fisher who left illegal fishing to work in environmental conservation tells Mada Masr on condition of anonymity. “A crocodile only attacks when it’s hungry or if someone gets close to its eggs or young,” they say. For fishers, especially Nubians, the relationship with crocodiles is, for the most part, one of coexistence. Before casting their net, a fisher would “feed the crocodile two big fish for good luck,” and it would then move away from their fishing site, the fisher adds. Large crocodiles are only killed if they attack fishing camps, a Nubian fisher who catches and trades crocodiles says to Mada Masr on condition of anonymity. Smaller ones are sometimes accidentally caught in fishing nets. Some are released back into the lake, others are mummified and hung above the doors of Nubian homes, where they are believed to bring good luck, they add. And if fishers are forced to kill a male crocodile after an attack, they would dry its testicles for use in folk practices associated with male virility, Mansour says. As for keeping live crocodiles in Nubian homes, the Nubian fisher says it began as a hobby, keeping up with a cultural tradition. In the early 1990s, however, after armed Islamist violence in Upper Egypt dealt a heavy blow to tourism, live crocodiles became a way to attract the few visitors who still visited Nubian villages. Mohamed Nabil, who works in the tourism sector in the Nubian village of West Soheil, says families began displaying crocodiles in small concrete pools in their homes as part of the “Nubian identity.” Though he disputes the idea that keeping live crocodiles was ever a Nubian tradition, either in West Soheil or any other village. Nabil, whose family owns several guesthouses in West Soheil, says that in the beginning, only three homes in the village kept crocodiles as a hobby. By the end of the 1990s, the practice had spread rapidly. Having a crocodile became a major part of the appeal of visiting a Nubian home. In any case, none of this, the Nubian fisher says, amounted to organized commercial activity until the crocodile trade later expanded beyond Aswan. According to Nabil, the trade began to take shape during the tourism downturn of the 1990s, when tourist bazaar owners started selling young crocodiles to attract visitors. The trade didn’t remain confined to Aswan for long. Crocodiles soon began making their way into Cairo’s wild animals markets, eventually turning up in irrigation canals across the Nile Delta. In 2003, young crocodiles appeared in Lake Manzala and the Damietta branch of the Nile after attacking fishers. Local residents blamed the incident on young men in the village of Tabl, in Damietta, who had been raising crocodiles in their homes. Then, in 2006, a two-meter crocodile escaped from workers at a restaurant in Giza’s Pharaonic Village as they were transporting four crocodiles the owner had bought to entertain visitors. Since then, crocodiles have continued to turn up far from Lake Nasser almost every year. According to a Mada Masr count based on Egyptian newspaper and website archives, Nile crocodiles have appeared 30 times across 13 governorates between 2003 and June this year. There has been one recorded sighting in each of Cairo, Alexandria, Sharqiya, Gharbiya, Beni Suef, Matrouh and New Valley; two in each of Monufiya and Kafr al-Sheikh; and three in Qalyubiya. Aswan recorded four incidents between 2010 and 2013, followed by one more in 2017. Nabil suggests some of these Aswan cases were likely linked to foreign tourists wanting to release young crocodiles into the Nile, which, he says, is the reason sightings tend to occur during the tourist season, between September and April. Giza has seen the highest number of sightings, with nine recorded cases. Much of that can be traced to Abu Rawash, a village in Kerdasa that has for more than a century been known for catching, raising and trading wild animals, particularly reptiles. People from Abu Rawash have traveled across Egypt collecting wild animals, with some later establishing partnerships with agriculture, science, veterinary medicine and pharmacy faculties at Egyptian and Arab universities, as well as universities in Asia and Europe. They also supply snakes and scorpions to VACSERA, one of the oldest Egyptian vaccine manufacturers. Two families obtained licenses to operate small zoos that host university and school visits. But everyone involved in the crocodile trade who spoke to Mada Masr says Abu Rawash’s declared business conceals a much larger trade in wild animals, including 14 species whose sale is prohibited under Egypt’s environmental law and the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species in Wild Flora and Fauna (CITES), to which Egypt is a signatory. From Abu Rawash, these animals make their way to public Friday markets in Cairo and Alexandria. The Nubian fisher describes Abu Rawash as “the biggest crocodile market for traders, not hobbyists.” For those at the very start of the supply chain, the profits are modest. The fisher says a crocodile was sold for just LE10 in the 1990s, when the trade was still controlled by only two or three traders in Aswan. It later expanded with the spread of home hatcheries for crocodile eggs. Today, a crocodile leaves Aswan for just around LE100 before passing through wholesalers in places like Abu Rawash and eventually reaching vendors. At Cairo’s Friday market in Sayeda Aisha, Mada Masr found one vendor selling a crocodile for LE1,500. They said most buyers purchase them to keep as pets. The hunt begins each year in late June, when crocodile eggs hatch around the shores of Lake Nasser, environmental researcher Amr al-Hady, who spent years working on the lake, tells Mada Masr. Female crocodiles bury around 40-50 eggs in sandy nesting sites along the inlets, in what fishers call the hatta . In the wild, natural predation leads to only 5-10 percent surviving. Removing eggs from these nests, thus, becomes a direct threat to the very existence of the species in the lake, Hady says. Omar Manjouneh, the director of Crocodopolis, an upcoming documentary which explores the crocodiles of Lake Nasser, says the film’s research found that the animals’ behavior has begun to shift in response to the changing factors around them. Unlike their historical behavior, Lake Nasser’s crocodiles now show signs of fear toward humans, diving underwater as soon as they spot a boat or hear its engine. They have also begun showing a tendency to migrate in an apparent attempt to escape poaching, even though they are not naturally a migratory species. Mansour, the fisher on Lake Nasser, argues that the state knows there is a crocodile trading market but has chosen to ban the practice and pursue people found with young crocodiles instead of regulating the trade. He believes the government should allow limited hunting during designated seasons and license crocodile farms for trade purposes, which, he says, would curb overhunting while giving fishers a legal source of income instead of forcing the trade underground. “The government only studies things on paper. It never consults the people who actually live here,” Mansour says. The idea of turning crocodiles into a regulated economic resource is not new. Even after the floods of the 1990s and the accompanying surge in the species’ population had subsided, government agencies repeatedly explored the possibility. In 2007, the agriculture and environment ministries launched a joint study to estimate crocodile numbers in Lake Nasser using satellite imagery, with the aim of determining how many could be commercially exploited without upsetting the lake’s ecological balance. The survey, which ran until 2013, estimated the population at between 2,200-3,000 animals capable of breeding, although some researchers believe those numbers have since fallen due to increased fishing, lower flood levels and expanding agricultural and industrial activities around the lake. While the study was underway, Egypt sought to change the crocodile’s status under CITES. In 2010, Egypt submitted a proposal to the convention’s 15th Conference of the Parties, seeking to move the Nile crocodile from Appendix I, which prohibits commercial trade in endangered species, to Appendix II, which covers species that are not threatened with extinction but could become so if trade is left unregulated. The change would have allowed Egypt to establish licensed crocodile farms and export crocodile skins and other products, and have part of production in Lake Nasser itself. The government proposed an initial annual export quota of 750 crocodile skins, presenting the project as a way to create jobs for communities around the lake while helping curb illegal hunting. The proposal won the support of 60 countries, but 38 opposed it, led by European states that questioned whether Egypt had adequate management and oversight in place. The conference ultimately agreed to move the Nile crocodile to Appendix II but with a zero commercial export quota until Egypt meets a series of conditions, including conducting scientific research, thus leaving the plans for commercial crocodile farms on hold. The idea resurfaced in early 2016, when then-Environment Minister Khaled Fahmy announced that the ministry was studying the establishment of a pilot crocodile farm. It later signed a cooperation protocol with the General Authority for Fish Resources Development to build a 50-feddan farm two km from Lake Nasser. But three years ago, the head of the Crocodile Management Unit at the Egyptian Environmental Affairs Agency in Aswan told Al-Ahram that the land had never been allocated because no authority had been assigned responsibility for it. Another attempt followed in 2019, when the Agriculture Ministry announced licensing requirements for crocodile farms. No applications were submitted, however, and the government has not announced any further developments. According to Abassiry, the biggest obstacle to establishing crocodile farms is the high cost of the studies needed before a project can begin, making investors reluctant to commit, especially since returns could take years to materialize. With every official attempt stalling, crocodiles have continued to leave Lake Nasser through the informal trade. The market expanded in the security vacuum that followed the January 2011 revolution. Authorities tried to curb it by arresting people found with crocodiles at airports and ports, tracking down sellers on social media, confiscating stuffed crocodiles from bazaars and returning live ones to Lake Nasser. But while the state kept returning the crocodiles to the lake, the route by which they were leaving it remained open. And Nile crocodiles continued to turn up in Delta irrigation canals and along the Mediterranean. Hady argues that if the state moves ahead with crocodile farming, it should also legalize the keeping of crocodiles in Nubian homes in West Soheil rather than leaving the practice outside any regulatory framework. Doing so, he says, would help preserve a tourism activity that has become part of many visitors’ experience, while also bringing the practice under the supervision of the Environment Ministry, helping curb overhunting and ensuring better animal welfare, particularly as some foreign tourists have become increasingly critical of the conditions in which crocodiles are kept in Nubian homes. The fisher who left illegal fishing to work in conservation says those initiatives have recently tried to reduce overhunting by educating fishers and persuading them into collecting only some of the nests in each inlet and leaving the rest undisturbed, while also refraining from catching large crocodiles. The idea, they say, is that protecting crocodiles will only work if it also serves the fisher’s own interests. “We’ve started telling them: the big crocodile you catch today is the eggs you’ll lose next year.” The post The unregulated crocodile first appeared on Mada Masr .

items.title: Pakistan ready to extend cooperation with Iran in all fields, Naqvi tells Iranian counterpart
items.description: Interior Minister Mohsin Naqvi said on Wednesday that Pakistan was ready to extend cooperation with Iran in all fields. He made the remarks as he met with Iranian Interior Minister Eskandar Momeni, who is currently in Pakistan on an official visit. A smartly turned-out contingent of the Federal Constabulary presented a guard of honour to Momeni upon his arrival at the interior ministry, according to a statement by the ministry. It said that Naqvi and his Iranian counterpart held one-on-one talks, followed by delegation-level discussions. It said that the two ministers reviewed bilateral relations, trade, regional developments and avenues for cooperation. According to the interior ministry, both sides agreed to enhance cooperation in counter-terrorism, counter-narcotics, prevention of illegal immigration and efforts against human trafficking. The two ministers also emphasised the need for joint efforts to curb drug smuggling and terrorism “originating from the Afghan border”, while agreeing to improve coordination between the relevant institutions of both countries. “Pakistan is ready to increase cooperation with Iran in all fields,” Naqvi was quoted as saying. He also praised the role of Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi and Momeni, saying they had made “extraordinary efforts for peace”. “I hope good news will come soon and peace will be established,” he added. Railway cooperation Later, Momeni also held a meeting with Railways Minister Hanif Abbasi, where the two sides agreed to expand railway cooperation and strengthen cross-border connectivity, state media reported. An official statement carried by state-run APP said that Iran’s ambassador to Pakistan, Reza Amiri Moghaddam, also attended the meeting. During the discussion, Momeni and Abbasi “reaffirmed their commitment to further strengthen religious, historical and cultural ties between Pakistan and Iran”. They also stressed the need for peace, stability, cooperation and enhanced diplomatic engagement in the region, the statement said, adding that the two sides held detailed discussions on railway cooperation, cross-border links and promotion of bilateral trade. Abbasi said work to upgrade Main Line-3 (ML-3) was expected to begin in the current fiscal year, with the project likely to be completed by December 2029, the statement said. It further quoted Abbasi as saying that ML-3 was a “key corridor” for railway connectivity between Pakistan and Iran and its upgradation would significantly boost trade and regional linkages. The minister appreciated the Iranian government and railways department for their efforts to rehabilitate the Zahedan-Taftan railway section. He said that at the request of the Iranian railways department, the Taftan Railway Station had been declared a customs clearance station, which would further facilitate cross-border freight and shunting operations. The meeting also discussed the revival of the Islamabad-Tehran-Istanbul (ITI) freight train service and measures to enhance regional connectivity, the statement said. It added that both sides also agreed to further expand railway cooperation under the 1959 bilateral railway agreement and the 2025 Framework of Cooperation. The two leaders expressed resolve to further strengthen cooperation between Pakistan and Iran in the area of rail transport, trade, border connectivity and economic collaboration, the statement said. A Momeni-led Iranian delegation also visited the National Emergencies Operations Centre (NEOC) at the National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) headquarters in Islamabad. During the visit, Momeni expressed Tehran’s interest in learning from Pakistan’s experience and knowledge-sharing, particularly in the areas of early warning systems, anticipatory disaster management, institutional coordination, and the application of advanced technologies for disaster risk reduction, an official statement said. Both sides underscored the importance of strengthening institutional linkages and promoting cooperation to address emerging risks associated with climate change and complex disasters, the report said. Momeni had arrived in Pakistan on an official visit on Monday night. On Tuesday, he met Chief of Defence Forces and Chief of the Army Staff Field Marshal Syed Asim Munir at General Headquarters in Rawalpindi, where matters of mutual interest, regional and bilateral security, and effective border management were discussed, according to Inter-Services Public Relations. He also met Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif in Islamabad, where the premier expressed concern over the escalation in the Middle East and reiterated Pakistan’s willingness to play its role as an “honest and sincere mediator and facilitator” to promote regional peace. Momeni’s visit comes as mediator Pakistan continues its diplomatic efforts to rescue the Islamabad Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) signed last month between the US and Iran, intended to end the conflict between the two sides that began with US-Israeli attacks on Iran in late February.

items.description: El hombre que presuntamente mató a Facundo Rico en una lujosa urbanización de Madrid la semana pasada se aplicó a sí mismo justicia antes de que lo hicieran los tribunales. A. J., el presunto autor del crimen, se suicidó el día después de asestar 13 puñaladas a este ingeniero de origen argentino con el que sospechaba que su mujer tenía una aventura. La investigación policial apunta a que el homicida vivía obsesionado con esos cuernos y que, ese día, acudió al domicilio de Facundo dispuesto a todo. Seguir leyendo

items.title: UNESCO renews concern over damage to Golestan Palace in Busan session
items.description: TEHRAN – UNESCO has renewed concern over damage to Tehran's Golestan Palace caused by the shock wave and debris from nearby military strikes, as the protection of cultural heritage during armed conflict returned to the agenda of the 48th session of the World Heritage Committee in Busan, South Korea.

items.title: UNESCO-listed Bisotun prepares to receive Arbaeen pilgrims, official says
items.description: TEHRAN – The UNESCO-listed Bisotun archaeological site has completed preparations to receive pilgrims travelling to Iraq for Arbaeen commemorations, with authorities planning to hold a handicrafts market along the pilgrimage route, the site’s executive director said on Wednesday.

items.title: Floor coverings in Iran: From Neolithic mats to Safavid silk carpets
items.description: TEHRAN – During the Neolithic period, the inhabitants of Iranian plateau used reed mats as floor coverings in their shelters and also wrapped the deceased in them for burial. Thousands of years later, during the Safavid era, the use of silk brocade carpets woven by the artisans of Tabriz became widespread.

items.title: Oudalan/CCCo: des acteurs locaux sensibilisés sur la loi « Faso Bu Kaoré », les libertés religieuses et la sécurité routière
items.description: BURKINA-OUDALAN-CONCERTATION-REFORMES-SECURITE-ROUTIERE Oudalan/CCCo: des acteurs locaux sensibilisés sur la loi « Faso Bu Kaoré », les libertés religieuses et la sécurité routière Gorom-Gorom, 21 juil. 2026 (AIB) – Les acteurs locaux de la commune de Gorom-Gorom, réunis mardi à l'occasion de la première session ordinaire du Cadre de concertation communale (CCCo), ont été sensibilisés sur la loi « Faso Bu Kaoré », les libertés religieuses et la sécurité routière, avant de formuler des recommandations en vue d'une meilleure appropriation des réformes et de la prévention des accidents de la route. La rencontre a réuni les membres de la Délégation spéciale ainsi que les principales autorités administratives, judiciaires, coutumières, religieuses et les représentants de la société civile. Après le discours d'ouverture du PDS, le procureur du TGI de Dori, Kafando Yamtarba, a présenté la loi n°003-2026, dite « Faso Bu Kaoré », qui privilégie le règlement à l'amiable de certains différends en s'appuyant sur les us et coutumes. Selon lui, cette réforme vise à rapprocher la justice des citoyens, à valoriser les mécanismes traditionnels de règlement des conflits et à désengorger les juridictions. Dans une seconde communication, le magistrat est revenu sur la loi relative aux libertés religieuses. Il a insisté sur le respect de la laïcité de l'État, la promotion de la tolérance, du vivre-ensemble et de la cohésion sociale, tout en rappelant l'interdiction des discours de haine et de toute discrimination fondée sur la religion. Présentant la communication sur la sécurité routière, le lieutenant de police Zongo Daouda a indiqué que les accidents de la circulation à Gorom-Gorom sont principalement causés par l'excès de vitesse, les acrobaties, l'imprudence et le non-respect du Code de la route. À l'issue des échanges, plusieurs recommandations ont été formulées. Le directeur provincial de la Police nationale (DPPN), Daouda Sawadogo, a préconisé le renforcement de la sensibilisation des usagers et l'application rigoureuse de la loi. Le PDS, Sibiri Neya, a proposé de rendre obligatoire, pour les organisateurs de mariages sollicitant une autorisation de spectacle, la sécurisation de leurs cérémonies par les Forces de défense et de sécurité (FDS), à leurs frais, afin de prévenir les accidents. Pour sa part, le procureur Kafando Yamtarba a insisté sur la responsabilisation des mariés en cas d'accident. Les participants ont enfin recommandé d'intensifier les actions de sensibilisation afin de favoriser l'appropriation des nouvelles réformes et de renforcer la sécurité routière dans la commune. Agence d'information du Burkina DAM/bak BURKINA-OUDALAN-CONCERTATION-REFORMES-SECURITE-ROUTIERE Oudalan/CCCo: des acteurs locaux sensibilisés sur la loi « Faso Bu Kaoré », les libertés religieuses et la sécurité routière Gorom-Gorom, 21 juil. 2026 (AIB) – Les acteurs locaux de la commune de Gorom-Gorom, réunis mardi à l'occasion de la première session ordinaire du Cadre de concertation communale (CCCo), ont été sensibilisés sur la loi « Faso Bu Kaoré », les libertés religieuses et la sécurité routière, avant de formuler des recommandations en vue d'une meilleure appropriation des réformes et de la prévention des accidents de la route. items.title: Djôrô: Le gouverneur Siaka Barro exhorte les nouveaux bacheliers de la région à opérer des choix de filières réfléchis
items.description: BURKINA-DJÔRÔ-ENSEIGNEMENT-SUPÉRIEUR-ORIENTATION-UNIVERSITAIRE Djôrô: Le gouverneur Siaka Barro exhorte les nouveaux bacheliers de la région à opérer des choix de filières réfléchis Gaoua, 21 juil. 2026 (AIB) – Le gouverneur de la région du Djôrô, Siaka Barro, a exhorté les nouveaux bacheliers à faire des choix de filières mûrement réfléchis, en adéquation avec leurs compétences, leurs aspirations et les besoins du Burkina Faso, lors d’un atelier d’information et d’orientation organisé lundi à Gaoua à leur profit par la Direction régionale de l’Enseignement supérieur, de la Recherche et de l’Innovation (DRESRI) du Djôrô. L’activité a permis de présenter les différentes offres de formation des établissements d’enseignement supérieur et de sensibiliser les futurs étudiants à l’importance d’un choix de filière éclairé et adapté aux besoins du pays. À cette occasion, le gouverneur du Djôrô, Siaka Barro, a félicité les nouveaux admis au baccalauréat et les a exhortés à faire des choix de filières mûrement réfléchis, en adéquation avec leurs compétences, leurs aspirations et les besoins du Burkina Faso. Le gouverneur a insisté sur la nécessité pour les nouveaux bacheliers de bénéficier des conseils de spécialistes et de l’accompagnement de leurs parents avant tout choix d’orientation. Il a également souligné qu’une orientation réussie constitue un facteur déterminant pour la réussite académique et l’insertion professionnelle des étudiants. Selon le directeur régional de l’Enseignement supérieur, de la Recherche et de l’Innovation du Djôrô, Raogo Noël Gansonré, cette activité s’inscrit dans le prolongement de la Semaine nationale de l’information et de l’orientation initiée par le ministère en charge de l’Enseignement supérieur. Pour lui, elle vise à rapprocher les nouveaux bacheliers des différentes régions du Burkina Faso des informations relatives aux offres de formation disponibles. « Les participants ont été informés des opportunités offertes aussi bien par les universités publiques que privées du pays. Les communications ont notamment porté sur les différentes unités de formation et de recherche, ainsi que sur les débouchés professionnels des filières présentées », a-t-il ajouté. Agence d’information du Burkina MS/bak

items.title: Cameroun: le parti au pouvoir répond à la controverse sur l'absence du président depuis plus de 40 jours
items.description: « Il n'y a aucune vacance de pouvoir au sommet de l'État », a affirmé Jacques Fame Ndongo, ministre d'État et secrétaire national à la communication du Rassemblement démocratique du peuple camerounais (RDPC), le parti présidentiel. Sortie faite le 21 juillet, qui intervient en pleine controverse sur l'absence prolongée du chef de l’État, hors de son pays depuis quarante-cinq jours. Mais plutôt que de l'éteindre, la controverse est repartie de plus belle.

items.title: Hommage à Nelson Mandela: l’ONU appelle à faire reculer les inégalités
items.description: Les Nations Unies ont rendu hommage, lundi, à l'ancien président sud-africain, Nelson Mandela, tout en appelant à remettre la lutte contre la pauvreté et les inégalités au premier rang des priorités mondiales. Face à la multiplication des conflits, au recul de l'aide au développement et à l'envolée des dépenses militaires, le Secrétaire général de l'ONU a estimé que le monde faisait aujourd'hui « les mauvais choix ». Dans un discours prononcé par son Chef de cabinet, Earle Courtenay Rattray, devant l'Assemblée générale des Nations Unies, António Guterres a salué la mémoire de Mandela, symbole de paix, de justice et de défense des droits humains. « Même dans les moments les plus sombres de sa vie, Nelson Mandela comprenait que le désespoir était un luxe que l'humanité ne pouvait pas se permettre », a rappelé le Secrétaire général. Un héritage porteur d'espoir M. Guterres a souligné que Mandela avait consacré son existence à combattre l'apartheid en Afrique du Sud, tout en dénonçant les injustices héritées du colonialisme, du racisme et de la traite transatlantique des personnes réduites en esclavage. Son combat, a-t-il dit, continue d'inspirer ceux qui défendent les droits humains et l'égalité dans le monde. Évoquant sa visite de la cellule où Mandela passa dix-huit de ses vingt-sept années de prison sur l'île de Robben Island, M. Guterres a dit avoir été profondément marqué par « la résilience de son esprit » et par sa capacité à transformer la souffrance en réconciliation. Le thème retenu cette année pour la Journée internationale Nelson Mandela met l'accent sur la lutte contre la pauvreté et les inégalités, un défi que le chef de l'ONU juge plus urgent que jamais. « Aujourd'hui, nous vivons dans un monde où les inégalités se sont enracinées », a-t-il averti, soulignant que des centaines de millions de personnes restent privées d'un accès suffisant à l'alimentation, à l'eau, aux soins de santé et à l'éducation, tandis que les conflits, les crises économiques et le changement climatique aggravent leur situation. Un monde marqué par des inégalités persistantes Photo de l'ONU/Loey Felipe Courtenay Rattray, chef de cabinet du Secrétaire général de l'ONU, s'adresse à l'Assemblée générale à l'occasion de la célébration de la Journée internationale Nelson Mandela. Pour António Guterres, cette réalité est d'autant plus préoccupante que les besoins humanitaires augmentent alors que l'aide internationale diminue. « Il y a quelque chose de profondément anormal lorsque nous dépensons davantage pour les instruments de destruction et de mort que pour les outils du développement et de la paix », a-t-il déclaré. S'appuyant sur la célèbre phrase de Nelson Mandela selon laquelle « Comme l'esclavage et l'apartheid, la pauvreté n'est pas naturelle », le Secrétaire général a appelé à « faire de meilleurs choix » en investissant davantage dans le développement, la protection sociale, la santé, l'éducation et les pays les plus vulnérables, plutôt que dans les dépenses militaires. Agir contre la pauvreté La Présidente de la 80e session de l’Assemblée générale des Nations Unies, Annalena Baerbock, a aussi appelé les États membres à transformer l’héritage de l’ancien dirigeant sud-africain en actions concrètes contre la pauvreté et les inégalités. Rappelant le parcours de Mandela, elle a souligné sa capacité à choisir « le dialogue plutôt que la haine » et la réconciliation après des décennies d’injustice. Elle a alerté sur l’ampleur du défi actuel : 808 millions de personnes vivent encore dans l’extrême pauvreté et seuls un pays sur cinq est en voie de réduire de moitié son taux national de pauvreté d’ici à 2030. Alors que l’échéance des Objectifs de développement durable (ODD) approche, elle a appelé la communauté internationale à agir sans attendre : « C’est encore entre nos mains ». UN Photo/Loey Felipe La Présidente de l'Assemblée générale des Nations Unies, Annalena Baerbock, à l'occasion de la célébration de la Journée internationale Nelson Mandela. 67 minutes au service de sa communauté Créée par l'Assemblée générale des Nations Unies, la Journée internationale Nelson Mandela est célébrée chaque année autour du 18 juillet, date de naissance de l'ancien président sud-africain. Elle invite chacun à consacrer symboliquement 67 minutes au service de sa communauté, en hommage aux 67 années que Madiba a consacrées à la lutte pour la justice. « Vaincre la pauvreté et les inégalités n'est pas un acte de charité, mais un acte de justice », a conclu António Guterres, invitant chacun à faire vivre les valeurs de liberté, d'égalité, de solidarité, de réconciliation et de paix incarnées par Nelson Mandela. Source: ONU Info L’article Hommage à Nelson Mandela: l’ONU appelle à faire reculer les inégalités est apparu en premier sur Wakat Séra .

items.title: Séno/CSAPé 2026 : 34 professeurs des écoles composent à Dori pour accéder au grade de PCE
items.description: Burkina-Liptako-Éducation-Examen-Professionnel Séno/CSAPé 2026 : 34 professeurs des écoles composent à Dori pour accéder au grade de PCE Dori, 21 juil. 2026 (AIB) – Trente-quatre professeurs des écoles (PE) ont pris part, mardi à Dori, aux épreuves écrites du Certificat supérieur d’aptitude pédagogique (CSAPé), session 2026. Cet examen professionnel constitue une étape décisive pour leur accession au grade de professeur certifié des écoles (PCE). Pour cette session, 66 candidats s’étaient inscrits au centre de Dori. Toutefois, seuls 34 candidats ont effectivement composé sur place. Les 32 autres ont choisi de passer les épreuves dans d’autres centres du pays, conformément aux dispositions prévues pour l’organisation de l’examen. Les épreuves ont été officiellement lancées à 7 h 20 par le directeur régional de l’Éducation préscolaire, primaire et non formelle (DREPPNF) du Liptako/Soum, Noufou Tao, à travers l’ouverture de l’enveloppe contenant les sujets. Le directeur régional a encouragé les candidats, tout en les invitant à faire preuve de sérénité et à respecter strictement les consignes régissant le déroulement de cet examen professionnel, afin d’en garantir la crédibilité et la transparence. Les épreuves ont porté notamment sur la pédagogie générale, la psychopédagogie, l’administration et la gestion scolaires, ainsi que sur la didactique des différentes disciplines enseignées à l’école primaire. La réussite à cet examen permettra aux candidats admis d’accéder au grade de professeur certifié des écoles, avec des responsabilités pédagogiques accrues au service de l’amélioration des apprentissages. Agence d’information du Burkina AMM/bak

items.title: Iran marks World Zoonoses Day
items.description: TEHRAN – Representatives from the Ministry of Health and Medical Education, the Veterinary Organization of Iran, the Department of Environment, municipalities, universities, and the World Health Organization (WHO) got together in Tehran on Tuesday to observe World Zoonoses Day 2026.

items.title: Albayrak déboutée en appel : 526 ex-employés obtiennent 8 millions GNF chacun
items.description: Nouveau revers judiciaire pour la société Albayrak. La Cour d'appel de Conakry a confirmé, ce mardi 21 juillet 2026, le jugement rendu le 19 février dernier par le Tribunal de première instance (TPI) de Mafanco dans le litige l'opposant à ses 526 anciens employés. Présidée par le magistrat Noël Kolomou, la juridiction d'appel a estimé que les arguments développés par la société n'étaient pas de nature à infirmer la décision de première instance. Elle a donc confirmé l'intégralité du jugement entrepris. En outre, la Cour a condamné Albayrak à verser à chacun des 526 anciens salariés une indemnité de 8 millions de francs guinéens pour appel jugé abusif. Elle a également assorti sa décision d'une astreinte d'un million de francs guinéens par jour de retard en cas de non-exécution. L'audience de délibéré, particulièrement attendue, s'est déroulée dans une salle d'audience bondée. De nombreux anciens employés avaient fait le déplacement pour assister au prononcé de la décision, accueillie avec satisfaction par les intéressés. Cette affaire trouve son origine dans le licenciement collectif de 526 travailleurs de la société Albayrak. Contestant la rupture de leurs contrats de travail, les ex-employés avaient saisi la justice afin d'obtenir réparation du préjudice subi. Au cours de la procédure, ils avaient évalué leurs prétentions à près de 14 milliards de francs guinéens. Avec cet arrêt, les anciens travailleurs enregistrent une nouvelle victoire judiciaire face à leur ex-employeur. Reste désormais à savoir si la société exécutera volontairement cette décision ou choisira d'exercer les voies de recours qui lui restent éventuellement ouvertes.

items.title: 4 boys arrested over wet tissue ball, water assaults on pedestrians in Tsuen Wan
items.description: Hong Kong police have arrested four boys as young as 12 years old after they allegedly threw wet tissue balls and splashed water at passers-by while cycling along Tsuen Wan’s waterfront and in a public park. Tsuen Wan Chief Inspector Leung Wai-yee said on Wednesday that the arrests followed the circulation of videos online showing the incidents that took place between July 18 and 20. The four boys, aged 12 to 14, were arrested in Tsuen Wan and Kwai Tsing on suspicion of common assault. The Form...

items.title: World’s first AI model to earn perfect score at maths Olympiad comes from China’s RedNote
items.description: For the first time, an artificial intelligence model has achieved a perfect score at the International Mathematical Olympiad (IMO) – and it was developed by Chinese social giant RedNote. The model, dots-note-3.0, solved all six puzzles and secured a perfect score of 42 at this year’s IMO, which concluded in Shanghai on Monday. The note variant, still in beta phase, is currently the lightest model in the dots3 family, which includes two larger versions – jazz and aria – tailored for different use...

items.title: Russia’s grinding war in Ukraine shows the impotence of nuclear weapons
items.description: Why haven’t Ukraine’s drone and missile attacks on Russia triggered Moscow’s use of tactical nuclear weapons, as it has threatened? When Russia is attacked time and again, one wonders what purpose it serves to have the world’s largest nuclear arsenal. The Ukraine war is at a stalemate. Though roughly 90 per cent of the territory in East Donbas is under Russian control, its ground advances have slowed to less than 100 metres a day, according to a July 1 report by the Centre for Strategic...
